title: Ready, Aim, Marry Me

jokes.text: What tipped you? The falling profits or that we're a regular feature on Bill O'Reilly's 'Most Ridiculous Item of the Day'?
jokes.setup: Lucille says she thinks the company is in trouble.
jokes.punchline: Michael sarcastically lists two absurd indicators of the company's decline — falling profits AND being a recurring segment on a cable news mockery reel.
jokes.explanation: Sharp, economical sarcasm that establishes the Bluth Company's absurd public humiliation. The Bill O'Reilly reference is period-specific but the underlying joke — that they're so ridiculous they make the news as a punchline — is well-constructed and character-appropriate for the sardonic Michael.

jokes.text: Lucille calls Lucille 2 'that bitch!' and Michael corrects her: 'You're not at home, Mom. She doesn't live next door when you're here.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is ranting about Lucille 2's supposed power grab.
jokes.punchline: Michael's correction implies Lucille routinely screams profanities at her neighbor through walls, and that she's carrying this habit to a new location — treating it as a spatial/logistical issue rather than a behavioral one.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully character-specific. The joke only works because of the established geography of the Bluths' penthouse and Lucille 2's adjacent apartment. Michael's correction treats a deeply antisocial behavior as a mundane spatial problem, which is both a perfect Lucille character moment and a perfect Michael straight-man moment. High character integration score because this joke literally could not work for any other characters on any other show.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'She's trying to prove she's closer to my children than I am — but the joke's on her, because she doesn't know how little I care for Gob.' Michael: 'Think that makes the joke on Gob.'
jokes.setup: Lucille smugly believes she has one-upped Lucille 2 by not caring about Gob.
jokes.punchline: Michael quietly redirects the cruelty: if Lucille doesn't care for Gob, Gob is the one being hurt, not Lucille 2.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy and character integration. In one short exchange, the show captures: Lucille's sociopathic self-satisfaction, her casual cruelty toward Gob, and Michael's weary moral clarity — all without any of them being aware of the full irony. The logic is airtight: Lucille celebrates her own indifference as a weapon, but Michael correctly identifies that the weapon faces inward. Could only exist between these two characters with this specific dynamic. Earns a high score.

jokes.text: George Michael asks if 'Gangy' had an accident, visibly concerned; Michael reassures him it was just her drink.
jokes.setup: George Michael walks in and notices something is wrong, asking if Gangy had an 'accident.'
jokes.punchline: The euphemism 'accident' implies he's asking if his grandmother soiled herself; Michael clarifies it was her drink — but the fact that this is a reasonable fear speaks volumes about Lucille.
jokes.explanation: A competent implied gag about Lucille's physical state being so deteriorated that a grandchild's first assumption is incontinence. Works through implication and character, though it's a fairly standard joke type for this show.

jokes.text: Michael tells George Michael the Valentine's basket is 'for us' — narrator immediately cuts to Michael at Sitwell Enterprises trying to set up a date with Sally.
jokes.setup: Michael earnestly tells his son the romantic basket is meant for father-son bonding, not for a date with Sally.
jokes.punchline: Narrator immediately reveals Michael was just at Sitwell's to set up a date with Sally.
jokes.explanation: Classic Arrested Development narrator irony construction — character states something earnestly, narrator immediately undercuts it with the truth. The structure is tight and the gap between Michael's self-deception and reality is satisfying. The comedic device is a show signature, so it loses some originality points, but it's executed with good economy.

jokes.text: Michael at Sitwell's: 'This is not for her. It's for my son. Just don't tell her I came by.' — The narrator reveals Michael was actually talking to Sally's father the whole time.
jokes.setup: Michael is covering his tracks with the person at Sitwell Enterprises, claiming the package is for his son.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals the person Michael was talking to was Sally's father, Stan Sitwell — making the 'don't tell her' instruction simultaneously embarrassing and moot.
jokes.explanation: The delayed reveal that Michael was confessing to the father rather than an assistant is structurally satisfying. It layers humiliation: Michael lied about the basket AND revealed his interest to exactly the wrong person. Rewatch bonus because you catch the setup more clearly on second viewing.

jokes.text: Stan Sitwell: 'I look like Edgar Winter. Forget it. I gotta get to spin class.'
jokes.setup: Stan Sitwell, who has alopecia (no hair, no eyebrows), is looking at something that apparently makes him look like albino rock musician Edgar Winter.
jokes.punchline: He self-deprecatingly invokes Edgar Winter and storms off to spin class.
jokes.explanation: The Edgar Winter visual callback (later confirmed by the narrator at 16:37) is a well-planted joke that pays off twice. The character specificity is high — this joke only exists because of Sitwell's alopecia, which is a running gag. Economy is strong. The narrator later explicitly calling it out is a bonus layer.

jokes.text: George Michael finds Kama Sutra oil in the Valentine's basket. Michael: '[Clicks Tongue] Maybe it's not for us.'
jokes.setup: Michael has been telling his son the romantic Valentine's basket is meant as wholesome father-son fun.
jokes.punchline: George Michael finds Kama Sutra oil in the basket, instantly revealing the basket was designed for an adult romantic context — and Michael awkwardly concedes with a click of his tongue.
jokes.explanation: Quick, clean, and lands because Michael has been insisting the basket is perfectly appropriate for father and son. The reversal is efficient. The '[Clicks Tongue]' physical beat adds a lot — it's the sound of a man realizing he has miscalculated. Good economy.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I'm afraid I prematurely shot my wad on what was supposed to be a dry run, if you will. So now I'm afraid I have something of a mess on my hands.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is explaining that he previewed the romance package before Lindsay could experience it.
jokes.punchline: His description of the situation ('prematurely shot my wad,' 'dry run,' 'mess on my hands') is a dense cluster of sexual double entendres delivered with complete sincerity about what he believes is a mundane situation.
jokes.explanation: Among the finest examples of Tobias's running gag of inadvertent sexual/homoerotic language. The phrase 'prematurely shot my wad on what was supposed to be a dry run' is extraordinarily well-crafted: it stacks three double entendres into a single coherent sentence that makes perfect literal sense in context. Michael's follow-up ('There are so many poorly chosen words in that sentence') is the perfect straight-man button. This is a benchmark Tobias moment — high originality because of the density of layering, exceptional character integration because ONLY Tobias delivers this, and earned because it flows naturally from plot. Approaches the calibration anchors for an 8-9 score.

jokes.text: Michael: 'It's just — there are so many poorly chosen words in that sentence.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has just delivered a sentence packed with sexual double entendres, completely unaware.
jokes.punchline: Michael's understatement ('so many poorly chosen words') is the perfect straight-man button — he doesn't enumerate them, doesn't explain, just acknowledges the catastrophe with exhausted precision.
jokes.explanation: A canonical Michael straight-man line. Its power comes from restraint — by NOT listing the problems, it forces the audience to do so themselves. 'So many' is the key word: it's both accurate and funnier than specificity. The economy score is near-perfect. Highly quotable in isolation and works as a button on one of the show's best Tobias moments.

jokes.text: Tobias goes to check out the romance package alone: finds body chocolate, moans, 'It's a wonderful package. I'll take it.' Attendant: 'You just did.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is sampling the body chocolate from the romance package in advance of his planned date with Lindsay.
jokes.punchline: The attendant's 'You just did' is a deadpan double entendre responding to 'I'll take it' — referring both to the chocolate purchase and to the fact that Tobias already consumed/took it.
jokes.explanation: A clean, quick double entendre from a background character that works because it's delivered deadpan and is structurally sound. The attendant's timing is perfect. Slightly diminished by being a fairly obvious setup once the scenario is established.

jokes.text: Tobias imagines Lucille shrieking 'Where's Tobias?' and narrates: 'Oh, he's just sharing a romantic horseback ride with Michael Bluth and they're dipping each other in—'
jokes.setup: Tobias is trying to make Lindsay jealous by suggesting she imagine him having a romantic time without her.
jokes.punchline: The romantic scenario he constructs involves him and Michael dipping each other in body chocolate — Tobias's obliviousness makes the homoerotic imagery completely unconscious and therefore funnier.
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias dramatic imagination moment — the voices he does, the elaborate scenario, the complete lack of self-awareness about the homoerotic subtext. The Lucille voice impression ('Where's Tobias?!' in a screeching register) is a nice physical comedy beat inferred from the transcript. The interrupted sentence ('dipping each other in—') is a perfect structural choice.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'You can zing your arrow into my buttocks anytime.'
jokes.setup: Michael has offered to play Cupid and ask Lindsay out on the date on Tobias's behalf.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's gratitude manifests as an invitation to be shot in the backside with a Cupid arrow — the homoerotic subtext is invisible to him.
jokes.explanation: Short, punchy, and extremely quotable. The joke is pure Tobias: the metaphor is innocent on its surface but the specific body part and the 'anytime' make it impossible not to read otherwise. High economy score for doing so much with so few words. High character integration because it's classically Tobias. Loses some originality points because inadvertent innuendo is Tobias's whole mode.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Buy yourself a tape recorder. Record yourself for a whole day. I think you're gonna be surprised at some of your phrasing.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has just invited Michael to shoot an arrow into his buttocks.
jokes.punchline: Michael's advice — record yourself for a day and listen back — foreshadows the tag scene where Tobias does exactly this and is horrified. It's both a punchline in the moment and a plant.
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly planted joke that pays off in the episode's tag scene. The advice is funny as a reaction to Tobias's inadvertent innuendo, but becomes significantly funnier on rewatch knowing the tape recorder becomes a real plot device. This is quintessential Arrested Development: a joke that's funny now and funnier later. Rewatch bonus is high.

jokes.text: George Sr. emerges from somewhere and says 'Butterscotch! Want a lick?' — implying he has a butterscotch candy.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is supposedly under house arrest, hiding in the attic. He appears suddenly mid-serious conversation.
jokes.punchline: His casual, cheerful 'Butterscotch! Want a lick?' after an earnest moment is pure absurdist character comedy — the contrast between the gravity of the family's situation and George Sr.'s candy obsession.
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s non sequitur candy moment is funny primarily through contrast and character — a patriarch under house arrest for fraud enthusiastically offering butterscotch candies. Brief and efficient.

jokes.text: Lindsay imitates a chicken at Michael for being too scared to ask Sally Sitwell on a date. Then Gob joins in. Then they're both doing chicken impressions at Michael.
jokes.setup: Lindsay accuses Michael of giving away his Valentine's date because he's afraid to ask Sally Sitwell.
jokes.punchline: She does the Bluth family's signature chicken impression ('Chaw-chee-chaw, chee-chaw'), Gob joins in, and Michael desperately insists 'These aren't even birds.'
jokes.explanation: One of the show's most beloved running gags. The chicken impression is funny because each Bluth does a different, objectively wrong version and believes it to be accurate. The gag escalates as Gob joins Lindsay. Michael's 'These aren't even birds' is the perfect button — a line that has become legendary among fans. High impact score because audiences who know the show erupt at this. High rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: Oh. I have to go with Tobias? / Lindsay, your husband really wants this to work. / Come on. Let me play Cupid this year. / I thought he went already.
jokes.setup: Michael is setting up the romantic getaway for Lindsay and Tobias as a Valentine's gift.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's deflated 'I have to go with Tobias?' followed by her attempt to get out of it via the technicality that Tobias already visited — and Michael's 'let me play Cupid' echoes the Tobias arrow-in-buttocks joke.
jokes.explanation: Michael's 'let me play Cupid' is a callback to the Tobias Cupid exchange minutes earlier. Lindsay's 'I thought he went already' is a logical trap Michael can't escape — Tobias did literally already go to scout the place. Character-specific and structurally tidy.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I'm playing the part of the gigolo so that she'll keep the company afloat.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Gob is seeing Lucille 2 and Gob is offended — but then explains he's deliberately leading her on for financial reasons.
jokes.punchline: Gob's self-justification — that he's playing the part of a gigolo for business purposes — is delivered with complete seriousness and no apparent shame.
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy — Gob's self-serving rationalization that he's being a gigolo for noble family reasons is perfectly in character. The word 'gigolo' delivered earnestly is inherently funny. Clean and efficient.

jokes.text: George Sr. gets himself wedged under the furnace chasing a sour ball that rolled out of his pocket.
jokes.setup: Michael says 'This is what Dad was really good at — getting us out of tight spots.' The narrator pivots to: 'In fact, at that very moment, George Sr. was in a tight spot...'
jokes.punchline: George Sr. has literally gotten himself into a tight spot — wedged under a furnace chasing a dropped sour ball candy.
jokes.explanation: A masterwork of the show's signature narrator-pivot joke construction. The phrase 'tight spot' is used idiomatically by Michael, then literalized absurdly by the narrator. The physical detail — it's a SOUR BALL, not a dollar or something valuable — perfectly calibrates George Sr.'s tragic absurdity. He's howling under a furnace over a piece of penny candy. The butterscotch callback from moments earlier makes the candy fixation a running character note. Structure is near-perfect: setup, pivot, revelation, absurd specific detail. One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: Hey, I'm— [Bleeps] Lucille 2. / Oh. I could do something like that.
jokes.setup: Michael lists what each family member contributes to the business; Gob defensively offers that he is 'sleeping with Lucille 2' as his contribution.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay immediately volunteers to do similar 'work' — treating sexual favors as a legitimate business function.
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's 'I could do something like that' — enthusiastic and immediate — is darkly funny because it reveals her standards for professional contribution. The bleeping on Gob's line makes the audience fill in the blank, which lands better than the explicit word.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'I think the answer to this problem is money. This may just be me having my head against a hundred-degree furnace for an hour, but maybe we should bring in Jack Dorso.'
jokes.setup: Michael is worried about Lucille 2 selling the company. George Sr. offers wisdom.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. qualifies his advice with 'this may just be me having my head against a hundred-degree furnace for an hour' — as if that's a reasonable disclaimer for what is actually basic advice.
jokes.explanation: The furnace detail is still warm (literally and comedically) from the previous joke. George Sr.'s casual acknowledgment that his thinking may be impaired — without actually stepping back from his advice — is perfect deadpan character comedy. The structure works because the disclaimer validates the absurdity of the previous scene while extending it.

jokes.text: The Jack Dorso origin story: radio/film serial 'Red McGibbon and Bullet: Nazi Hunters.' Bullet's catchphrase is 'Shoot me!' which everyone literally does — glass shatters, gunshot sounds.
jokes.setup: The narrator explains Jack Dorso's career in a B-serial called 'Red McGibbon and Bullet: Nazi Hunters.'
jokes.punchline: The clip shows 'Bullet' saying 'Shoot me!' and being shot — which is his literal catchphrase/command. 'Happy Valentine's Day, Nazis.'
jokes.explanation: A wonderful piece of show mythology construction. 'Shoot me!' as a literal command (rather than the idiom 'shoot me now') is a well-planted joke that will pay off repeatedly throughout the episode as Jack shouts it in the sitcom context where no one shoots him. 'Happy Valentine's Day, Nazis' is a perfect non sequitur button. The absurdist backstory is inventive and specifically calibrated.

jokes.text: [Bullet On Radio] Happy Valentine's Day, Nazis.
jokes.setup: The narrator is giving the backstory of Jack Dorso's career as Bullet in a Nazi-hunting radio serial.
jokes.punchline: The audio clip from the radio show has Bullet delivering 'Happy Valentine's Day, Nazis' before presumably opening fire — the incongruity of Valentine's sweetness with Nazi-hunting is perfectly absurd.
jokes.explanation: Five words that are pitch-perfect: the Valentine's holiday theme of the episode is weaponized in the most incongruous possible context. The cheerful formality of 'Happy Valentine's Day' directed at Nazis before violence is exactly the kind of absurd historical comedy the show loves. Extremely quotable out of context.

jokes.text: The narrator explains the audience wasn't ready to accept Bullet as a romantic lead 'despite his powerful upper body.'
jokes.setup: Jack Dorso attempted a solo romantic feature after his costar was jailed.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's deadpan defense of Bullet — that audiences were wrong not to accept him as a romantic lead because of his 'powerful upper body' — is funny because Jack Dorso has no lower body to speak of.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan advocacy for a half-man as a romantic lead is precisely calibrated absurdism. 'Despite his powerful upper body' is doing a lot of work — it implies that a powerful upper body should be sufficient for romance, which only makes sense if you've accepted the premise. Clean and economical.

jokes.text: Jack Dorso's annual birthday feat of strength: 'Five pounds for each year of my life.' He loses the use of his legs on his 70th birthday — 'Release!' [Cracking] 'Too much!'
jokes.setup: Jack Dorso became known for annual birthday feats of strength, lifting weight equal to five times his age.
jokes.punchline: On his 70th birthday he lifted 350 pounds, which destroyed his legs — the moment is shown with sound effects: cracking and 'Too much!'
jokes.explanation: A perfectly constructed origin story for Jack's disability that's both tragic and funny. The math is the joke — the escalating weight scale means age itself defeats him. 'Release!' is a great character choice: even at the moment of self-destruction, he's in command mode. The cracking sound effect seals it. Rewatch bonus for fully appreciating the structural elegance.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'See if you can talk your mother into taking another bullet for the Bluth family.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is suggesting Lucille should seduce Jack Dorso to get his money.
jokes.punchline: The phrase 'taking another bullet' is both an idiom (sacrifice yourself) and a literal reference to Jack Dorso's character name 'Bullet' — a double meaning delivered casually.
jokes.explanation: Exemplary Arrested Development wordplay. The phrase 'taking a bullet' is an ordinary idiom for self-sacrifice, but here it simultaneously refers to Lucille sleeping with a man literally named Bullet. George Sr. may or may not be aware of the double meaning — and that ambiguity makes it richer. Extremely economical: one phrase does double duty. Strong rewatch bonus as the layers become clearer.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Buster. Nice of you to take a break from that common whore you've been dating.'
jokes.setup: Buster arrives at the family gathering.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's greeting to her son is to insult his girlfriend as a 'common whore' — not a question or pleasantry, just a direct attack.
jokes.explanation: Classic Lucille greeting — weaponized pleasantry. The joke is the form: she frames an attack as a compliment ('nice of you to take a break'). Good character integration but not especially original within Lucille's repertoire.

jokes.text: Buster defends Starla: 'She is not a whore, Mother. She's the new secretary of the Bluth Company. And yes, she's more experienced than a normal girl. But sometimes love should be terrifying.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has called Buster's girlfriend a 'common whore.'
jokes.punchline: Buster's defense accidentally confirms everything: she's 'more experienced than a normal girl' and 'sometimes love should be terrifying' — he means to rebut but ends up supporting the characterization.
jokes.explanation: 'Sometimes love should be terrifying' is a genuinely great Buster line — simultaneously a defense, a confession, and a deeply sad statement about his relationship history with Lucille Sr. It's funny because it's true from his perspective but wrong from everyone else's. Strong character integration because Buster's damaged psychology is the entire joke.

jokes.text: Buster has been using his 'army stealth training' to sneak in and out of Lucille 2's apartment — hiding behind doors, grunting 'Makin' that love.'
jokes.setup: Narrator says Buster has been using his army stealth training for... something.
jokes.punchline: What he's using elite military techniques for: sneaking into his girlfriend's apartment and whispering 'Makin' that love' while rattling a door handle.
jokes.explanation: Buster applying military-grade stealth techniques to courtship is a delightful character comedy beat. 'Makin' that love' is a perfectly off-key phrase — simultaneously juvenile and ominous. The door handle rattling suggests the stealth wasn't very stealthy. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Lucille 2's not a "B," Mother. She's kind, and she's caring, and she's certainly less experienced than that whore that I'm dating now.'
jokes.setup: Lucille complains about Buster dating Lucille 2. Buster defends Lucille 2.
jokes.punchline: While defending Lucille 2, Buster casually confirms he is currently dating someone he himself calls a 'whore' — and uses this as a compliment to Lucille 2 by comparison.
jokes.explanation: A structurally elegant callback: Buster defended Starla against the 'whore' label, then pivots to using it himself while defending Lucille 2. The logic is perfect Buster: he has fully internalized his mother's value judgments and now applies them unselfconsciously. 'Certainly less experienced than that whore that I'm dating now' is extraordinary — he's simultaneously insulting his current girlfriend and complimenting his ex, in a sentence structured as a defense of his mother's enemy. This requires very high character integration to land.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And perhaps it was screaming it out loud that made Buster realize that he missed Lucille 2.'
jokes.setup: Buster has just inadvertently revealed his complex emotional situation by calling his current girlfriend a whore while defending his ex.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's dry observation that saying it aloud triggered the realization is a perfect piece of understated character psychology.
jokes.explanation: A gentle narrator beat that works because it treats Buster's emotional revelation as an almost mechanical process — he had to hear it to know it. The understatement is characteristic of the show's narrator style and lands cleanly.

jokes.text: Jack Dorso announces himself by shouting from offscreen: 'Someone order 140 pounds of upper-body strength?'
jokes.setup: The family is waiting for their wealthy contact Jack Dorso to arrive.
jokes.punchline: He announces himself not by name but by his physical specs — and the number (140 lbs of upper body strength) implies he has no lower body weight to add.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional entrance line. In nine words, it tells us everything: Jack is vain, he identifies with his physical power, and the weight specification subtly establishes his disability. The sentence structure ('Someone order...?') mimics a delivery person, which is the right level of absurdity. Very quotable and immediately iconic as a character introduction.

jokes.text: Well, he certainly sounds vital to me. / [Shouting] Walk, Dragon! Walk! / Enter the room! / He's half deaf. It was a stupid, stupid hire.
jokes.setup: Lucille is charmed by Jack's energetic self-announcement.
jokes.punchline: Jack then commands his giant assistant Dragon to walk, then shouts 'Enter the room!' — revealing both the absurd logistics of his existence and that Dragon is his legs. Michael's deadpan 'He's half deaf. It was a stupid, stupid hire' about... Dragon being deaf is the button.
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Jack's mobility depends on bellowing commands at a half-deaf giant is an absurdist visual comedy goldmine. Michael's aside about the hiring decision ('stupid, stupid hire') treats the entire absurd setup as a practical management problem.

jokes.text: Jack Dorso is announced as 'half deaf.' Lucille says it was 'a stupid, stupid hire.' Dragon mishears 'higher' and positions himself at eye level.
jokes.setup: Jack Dorso is carried by Dragon, his aide. Michael welcomes him.
jokes.punchline: Dragon is also nearly deaf and mishears 'higher' as a vertical instruction rather than a complaint about the hiring decision.
jokes.explanation: The wordplay on 'he's half deaf — stupid hire / higher' is a clean homophone gag that also establishes Dragon's deafness as a recurring dynamic. The joke functions on two levels simultaneously: it's funny that Dragon mishears, and funnier that he mishears in a way that's logistically useful. Good construction.

jokes.text: Jack Dorso gestures at the bridge mix: 'To the nuts!' Dragon brings him to face level with the bowl. 'The bridge mix! The bridge mix! Fool!'
jokes.setup: Jack Dorso wants some mixed nuts/bridge mix from a bowl.
jokes.punchline: Dragon misinterprets the command direction (perhaps taking it literally as 'to the nuts' meaning something else), bringing Jack to the nuts rather than the nuts to Jack — then gets berated. The correction 'The bridge mix! Fool!' is perfectly imperious.
jokes.explanation: Jack Dorso's imperious relationship with Dragon — barking commands that are somewhat ambiguous and then berating Dragon for the wrong interpretation — is a solid character comedy beat. 'Fool!' as a genuine exclamation is slightly anachronistic and adds to the absurdist quality.

jokes.text: Jack mistakes Buster for a war veteran and pins him with a surprise wrestling move, declaring 'I pinned him. I pinned the army man. God bless you for being in the army.'
jokes.setup: Jack sees Buster in his army uniform and is moved. He shouts 'Here comes Uncle Jack. Shoot me!' then attacks.
jokes.punchline: He physically pins Buster and celebrates it with genuine patriotic fervor, completely missing that this is a social gathering.
jokes.explanation: High physical comedy impact — a tiny elderly man with no functioning legs pins a full-grown Buster to the floor. The 'Shoot me!' catchphrase from the serial is now deployed in a domestic context where no one shoots him, which is the first running payoff of that planted joke. 'God bless you for being in the army' doubles the absurdity because Buster didn't actually serve in combat.

jokes.text: Is this the boy? Is this the little one? My God, you're an army man. / Well— [Chuckles] / I was never in the army. I was in the pictures.
jokes.setup: Jack meets Buster and immediately identifies him as 'an army man.'
jokes.punchline: Buster starts to accept the compliment, then Jack clarifies he means his own fictional army career in the movies — 'I was in the pictures' — completely ignoring Buster's actual military service.
jokes.explanation: Jack's solipsism — redirecting from Buster's real military service to his own fictional career — is a great character reveal. 'I was in the pictures' as a proud clarification that he never actually served is both self-aggrandizing and inadvertently insulting.

jokes.text: Jack sees Lucille (Sr.) and confuses her with Lucille 2, then is corrected. 'You look younger than you did the day I—' [Screams]
jokes.setup: Jack sees Lucille and thinks she's Lucille 2 (possibly an old flame).
jokes.punchline: When corrected that she's Lucille (Sr.), he screams in horror — implying his past with one of the Lucilles was romantic or intimate and this revelation is disturbing.
jokes.explanation: The interrupted sentence and scream works as a mystery/callback setup. The scream is a great physical beat — it implies a history with Lucille that is both romantic and horrifying. The joke also works as a compliment-turned-insult: he was mid-compliment when the identity correction horrified him.

jokes.text: Remember me? / Lucille. You look younger than you did the day l— / Oh, you old fool. I'm Lucille. / [Screams]
jokes.setup: Lucille approaches Jack, who appears to be about to reminisce warmly ('you look younger than you did the day I—').
jokes.punchline: Lucille interrupts: 'I'm Lucille' — implying he was confusing her with someone else (presumably she'd said her name). His scream suggests the new context is worse, not better.
jokes.explanation: The scream upon learning it's Lucille (rather than whoever he thought) is a good inversion — his horror at the real identity suggests the compliment was misapplied. The 'you old fool' from Lucille is appropriately wry.

jokes.text: Jack insists Lucille go 'back to the blonde. Back to the blonde.' — Apparently he has a physical preference from their past.
jokes.setup: Jack Dorso has screamed upon seeing Lucille Sr., apparently recognizing her from some past encounter.
jokes.punchline: His recovery is to give her unsolicited hair color instructions as though they have a previous intimate relationship he's still directing.
jokes.explanation: The implication that Jack has strong opinions about Lucille's hair — as though their relationship is ongoing and he has authority — is a subtle, dark character comedy beat. Less a laugh than a cringe of implied history.

jokes.text: Jack makes a gobbling noise and Michael comments 'He's kind of scary, that guy, huh?' George Michael: 'Not to me. I think he's kind of cool.'
jokes.setup: Jack Dorso is behaving erratically — gobbling noises, commands, wrestling.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's sincere assessment that Jack is 'kind of cool' is the kind of earnest, slightly-off evaluation that defines his character.
jokes.explanation: A small character beat that's true to George Michael's earnest-slightly-weird sensibility. Competent but slight — it's more character texture than a strong joke.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Well, why don't you go get your kiss from Mr. Cool Half-Man?' George Michael: 'I'd be fine with that.' — Then he goes over and Jack is shaken by Dragon and begins gurgling uncontrollably.
jokes.setup: Michael sarcastically suggests George Michael go kiss their 'cool' elderly visitor.
jokes.punchline: George Michael calls his bluff and goes — only for Jack to begin involuntarily gurgling because Dragon shook him, leading to the family crisis of 'Is he dying?'
jokes.explanation: A good escalation beat — the casual acceptance by George Michael is funny, and the immediate physical comedy of Jack gurgling is an effective escalation. The 'Is he dying?' panic is a nice button.

jokes.text: Ignore it. Just something the body does when you're shaken. [Gurgles] / Don't shake it! Don't shake it! / Towel! / Not the good ones!
jokes.setup: George Michael has shaken Jack's hand, and Jack begins gurgling/regurgitating from the motion.
jokes.punchline: The sequence escalates: Jack tries to calm everyone ('just something the body does'), then loses control ('Don't shake it!'), then demands a towel, then specifies 'Not the good ones!' — the final detail implying this happens regularly enough to have a towel protocol.
jokes.explanation: 'Not the good ones!' is the perfect button — it reveals that Jack has a towel hierarchy for his shaking incidents and has strong opinions about which towels are appropriate for bodily fluids. The escalation from 'ignore it' to 'NOT THE GOOD ONES' is a textbook comedy escalation beat.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Boy, that Dragon's cute, huh?' Lucille: 'What, the deaf giant who's holding our fake uncle?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is clearly attracted to Dragon.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's description — 'the deaf giant who's holding our fake uncle' — is technically accurate but so clinically specific that it's funnier than any insult. She reduces the situation to its barest absurd components.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's deadpan factual description of Dragon is unexpectedly funny because it strips all romance from Lindsay's attraction while being completely accurate. 'Our fake uncle' is particularly good — it reflects the narrator's recurring insistence on this clarification. High economy score: maximum information per word.

jokes.text: Lindsay: '...which would be a drag. [Snickers] Yeah, that's just the kind of joke Drag can't hear.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay says going with Dragon instead of Tobias 'would be a drag' — then realizes she made a pun.
jokes.punchline: She laughs at her own pun, then observes Dragon can't hear it anyway — which is both a commentary on the joke's quality and a gentle irony.
jokes.explanation: A self-aware pun that earns a bit of extra credit for the meta-observation that Dragon can't hear it. The joke is slight but the self-referential layer gives it a small upgrade. Lindsay's delight in her own weak pun is a good character beat.

jokes.text: Lindsay admits she never went to Sitwell as Michael asked. Michael: 'I give you one thing to do for the business, and you can't even do it.' Lindsay: 'I mean, if you'd tried and failed, I'd understand, but you didn't even try.' 'So I didn't even fail, and I don't see you giving me credit for that.'
jokes.setup: Michael is angry that Lindsay didn't follow through on her assignment.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's defense — that not trying means she didn't fail, which should be credited — is a logically perverse but internally consistent piece of her character's bottomless self-justification.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Lindsay's logic — not trying = not failing = deserves credit for a clean record — is a perfect crystallization of her character. It's internally consistent (not failing IS better than failing), which is what makes it more than just a selfish-character joke. The philosophical perversity is Arrested Development at its best: a character so committed to not contributing that she has developed a complete ethical framework around it. Genuinely quotable and highly character-specific.

jokes.text: Jack Dorso is perched on the edge of the building's balcony. Michael: 'Do you think you should be perched on the edge of the building like this?' Jack: 'Oh, Dragon's got me.' Dragon is in the bathroom.
jokes.setup: Jack is confidently balanced on a dangerous ledge, reassuring Michael that Dragon is holding him.
jokes.punchline: Dragon is in the bathroom. Jack immediately starts falling.
jokes.explanation: A perfect physical comedy setup — the immediate gap between Jack's confident assurance and the reality creates instant tension that pays off with slapstick. The bathroom detail is economical (explains Dragon's absence with maximum mundanity). 'Mama!' as Jack starts falling is a great character detail.

jokes.text: Jack describes Lindsay giving him 'the cutie eye.' 'Unfortunately that moose was holding me, so I couldn't make my move... Of course, I couldn't make that move without that moose, but that's my challenge.'
jokes.setup: Jack is explaining that he found Lindsay attractive at the party.
jokes.punchline: His frank acknowledgment that he couldn't pursue her because Dragon was holding him — then the admission that he also physically cannot pursue anyone without Dragon — is a darkly funny self-awareness about his limitations.
jokes.explanation: The self-contradiction — he couldn't pursue without Dragon, but also can't pursue because of Dragon — is the kind of elegant logical trap the show excels at. Jack's complete unselfconsciousness about his situation makes it dark without being mean. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Unfortunately that moose was holding me, so I couldn't make my move. / Of course I couldn't make that move without that moose, but that's my challenge.
jokes.setup: Jack is explaining why he couldn't flirt with Lindsay — Dragon (the 'moose') was holding him.
jokes.punchline: He acknowledges the paradox: Dragon prevented him from flirting, but he also can't move without Dragon. He frames this as simply 'my challenge' — a dignified acknowledgment of a logistical absurdity.
jokes.explanation: The paradox — Dragon enables Jack's movement but prevents his flirting — articulated with such calm dignity ('that's my challenge') is excellent character writing. Jack accepts the Sisyphean absurdity of his existence without complaint. The use of 'moose' for Dragon is also a funny character quirk.

jokes.text: Jack Dorso: 'I won't go wheelchair!'
jokes.setup: Jack Dorso has been holding on to the balcony while Michael tries to retrieve him.
jokes.punchline: His random declaration 'I won't go wheelchair!' — seemingly unprompted, as no one suggested a wheelchair — reveals a deep personal terror and life philosophy.
jokes.explanation: Four words that define an entire character. Jack Dorso has no legs, is dangling from a building, and his primary concern is maintaining his philosophical stance against wheelchairs. The comedy is in the priorities: survival is secondary to this principle. Maximum economy — not a single word is wasted. This is a legitimately great character comedy moment that approaches the highest tier for the episode.

jokes.text: Michael offers his sister to Jack Dorso for money. Narrator: 'Michael was torn. The business meant the world to him, but his sister's honor — ' Michael: 'Let's do it. Let's go share the good news.'
jokes.setup: The narrator sets up a moral dilemma — Michael must weigh business against his sister's honor.
jokes.punchline: Michael resolves the dilemma mid-narrator-sentence, cutting off any genuine deliberation. 'Let's do it' arrives before the narrator even finishes framing the conflict.
jokes.explanation: A structurally beautiful joke that uses the narrator against himself. The comic timing depends on Michael's line interrupting what is supposed to be a dramatic pause for moral reflection. It's a perfect deconstruction of the soap-opera 'torn hero' trope — Michael isn't torn at all, and the speed of his decision is the joke. The show's awareness of its own narrator conventions makes this meta without feeling smug.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael had offered his sister to his UncleJack in exchange for money to save the company. Remember, it's not their real uncle.'
jokes.setup: The narrator recaps that Michael has pimped out his sister to their 'uncle' for cash.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's legal/moral clarification — 'Remember, it's not their real uncle' — treats the incest concern as the main ethical issue, not the sex-for-money arrangement.
jokes.explanation: This iteration of the running gag is its best version because the context makes it darkest. The narrator worries about the incest angle of a prostitution-for-investment scheme — which is the correct ethical ladder in a show about a supremely amoral family. The prioritization is the joke. Very economical and well-structured.

jokes.text: [Inhales] He said he'd like to sleep on it.
jokes.setup: Lindsay asks what Jack said about bailing out the company financially.
jokes.punchline: Michael's significant pause before 'He said he'd like to sleep on it' — combined with the pause's implication that something more happened that Michael isn't sharing — is a deflation of expectation.
jokes.explanation: The 'inhale' before 'He said he'd like to sleep on it' suggests embarrassment or omission. Not a major joke but a solid character beat.

jokes.text: [Narrator] Meanwhile, Buster had decided to pursue a new girlfriend: His old girlfriend.
jokes.setup: The narrator teases that Buster has a new romantic target.
jokes.punchline: 'His old girlfriend' — the 'new' girlfriend is Lucille 2, who he was already dating before Starla. The flip from 'new' to 'old' in the space of one sentence is a classic narrator trick.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy: the narrator sets up 'new girlfriend' and immediately undercuts it with 'his old girlfriend' as a complete sentence. The rhythm of the delivery is ideal — the pause between sentences is the joke.

jokes.text: I'm keeping an eye on the woman who controls our company. / In her robe and slippies?
jokes.setup: Buster presents himself as conducting surveillance on Lucille 2 for business reasons.
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'In her robe and slippies?' punctures the espionage framing — Buster is clearly just pining outside his ex-girlfriend's apartment.
jokes.explanation: 'Robe and slippies' is a perfectly specific detail — not just 'pajamas' but the exact domestic attire that signals Buster is watching her in her private downtime. Gob's skepticism is well-earned. 'Slippies' as a word choice is endearingly childlike coming from Gob.

jokes.text: Buster: 'But I've lost her trail, and I don't think I have the skills to pick it up.' / Gob: 'Skills?' [grunts; demonstrates army moves]
jokes.setup: Buster says he doesn't have the skills for surveillance
jokes.punchline: Gob's wordless 'Skills?' followed by a demonstration of army moves is absurd because Gob is equally unqualified, and his confidence is total
jokes.explanation: A functional physical comedy beat. Gob's unearned confidence in skills he doesn't have is consistent with his character. The visual of the demonstration works better than the dialogue component.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'You are pimping out your sister?' Michael: 'You were pimping out my mother.' George Sr.: 'Yes, because she was sleeping with my brother, but your sister?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is horrified that Michael offered Lindsay to Jack Dorso in exchange for money.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s moral indignation is immediately deflated when Michael points out George did the same with Lucille — and George's defense is that his version was more justified because of the incest context.
jokes.explanation: This exchange is dark comedy at its sharpest. George Sr.'s position — that pimping out your wife is fine when she's sleeping with your brother, but pimping out your daughter is wrong — is internally logical within the Bluth family's moral universe. The hierarchical ethics (my version was worse circumstances therefore more acceptable) is perfect character comedy. Very dark, very earned, very Bluth.

jokes.text: You are pimping out your sister? / You were pimping out my mother. / Yes, because she was sleeping with my brother, but your sister?
jokes.setup: Michael tells his father he's arranged for Lindsay to spend Valentine's with Jack Dorso for money.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. is shocked — but Michael's counter ('you were pimping out my mother') stops him cold. George Sr.'s defense ('yes, because she was sleeping with my brother') reveals the full moral bankruptcy of the family's reasoning hierarchy.
jokes.explanation: The exchange is brilliant because George Sr.'s moral objection is immediately and correctly deflated by Michael, who points out that George has done exactly this. George Sr.'s defense — 'yes, because she was sleeping with my brother' — is both an admission and an attempt to distinguish cases by applying a completely absurd moral standard. It exposes the Bluth family's ethical framework with devastating efficiency. Highly quotable and completely character-specific.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I just screwed my brother-in-law.' Tobias appears: 'Well, I'm all grown up now.'
jokes.setup: Michael has realized he inadvertently hurt Tobias by setting up the romantic getaway without thinking through Tobias's role.
jokes.punchline: Tobias mishears or misunderstands 'screwed my brother-in-law' as a reference to something else — his 'I'm all grown up now' suggests he's acknowledging some kind of adult independence from the family.
jokes.explanation: A quick gag that works through Tobias's characteristic pattern of responding to things in unexpected ways. The non sequitur of 'I'm all grown up now' in response to Michael's self-recrimination is funny but slightly opaque — not the episode's strongest moment.

jokes.text: At the Four Seas Hotel, the horse coordinator Jeff monologues to Michael about the horse experience and juice: 'You'll meet him again at the end. Plus, he gets you your juice. Not your first juice, but the one you get at the spa.' Michael: 'That's right. You've done this before.' Jeff: 'But not with a lovely lady. Jeff doubts one's coming at all, don't you? Sorry. Sorry.'
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to maintain the pretense that a lady will arrive for his/Tobias's romantic package.
jokes.punchline: Jeff, a minor character, instantly sees through the deception and editorializes — first with 'Jeff doubts one's coming at all,' then with a reflexive apology. He refers to himself in the third person.
jokes.explanation: Jeff is a one-scene character who functions as a cringe comedy device — his third-person self-reference ('Jeff doubts one's coming at all') is an oddly formal and detached way to express judgment, which makes it funnier than a direct insult. The immediate apology doubles the cringe.

jokes.text: Tobias on horseback: 'You certainly didn't help with my reputation as a lady's man with Jeff. But we'll clear all that up in the spa when I get my facial.'
jokes.setup: Tobias and Michael are on horseback for the romantic getaway. No lady arrived.
jokes.punchline: Tobias is concerned about his 'reputation as a lady's man' with Jeff — a person he just met — and believes a spa facial will solve this. His priorities are perfectly inverted.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's concern about his lady's man status with a hotel horse wrangler is a good character beat — it shows his disconnect between self-perception and reality. The non sequitur of the facial as a reputation repair tool is typically Tobias.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Hey, did you ever get that tape recorder?' Tobias: '[Chuckles] You're funny. Come on, boy. Hoo-cha-cha!'
jokes.setup: Michael earlier recommended Tobias buy a tape recorder to hear his own phrasing.
jokes.punchline: Tobias laughs it off as Michael being funny — still completely unaware of his speech patterns — and then enthusiastically shouts 'Hoo-cha-cha!' at his horse, which is itself a mildly absurd non sequitur.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's dismissal of the tape recorder advice is the midpoint of a three-part joke arc (suggestion → dismissal → horrified compliance in the tag). His laugh ('You're funny') reveals total obliviousness. 'Hoo-cha-cha!' is an effectively absurd horse command. The rewatch bonus comes from knowing the tape recorder eventually gets used.

jokes.text: Jack Dorso: 'It's nice to do something you don't need legs for.' [Then he and Dragon fall off the horse with a massive splash/gurgling sound]
jokes.setup: Jack Dorso notes the pleasure of horseback riding as an activity accessible to him without legs.
jokes.punchline: He immediately falls off — which is both physically funny and ironic, since horseback riding does functionally require legs for balance.
jokes.explanation: Pitch-perfect comedic timing and irony — the line 'nice to do something you don't need legs for' creates an expectation of relief and inclusion, immediately subverted by a fall that proves legs are actually quite useful. The structure (setup, beat, fall) is immaculate. The line itself is genuinely funny even without the fall.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Lucille 2 showed up with Stan Sitwell, who did look a little like Edgar Winter.'
jokes.setup: Stan Sitwell arrives at the hotel romantic getaway with Lucille 2.
jokes.punchline: The narrator confirms what Stan said himself earlier — that his alopecia makes him look like Edgar Winter — with the casual factual delivery of a nature documentary.
jokes.explanation: The callback payoff of the Edgar Winter joke — now confirmed by the narrator's deadpan agreement. The economy of 'who did look a little like Edgar Winter' (introduced as a throwaway statement, now validated) is classic AD construction. The visual gag of seeing the alopecia in the spa context adds a layer.

jokes.text: The body chocolate callback: the attendant offers body chocolate to each couple in succession. Stan Sitwell: 'No.'
jokes.setup: Throughout the episode, body chocolate has been a featured item of the romance package. Now it's being offered to each pair at the spa.
jokes.punchline: Sitwell's flat 'No' — delivered without explanation — is both a comic beat and an implication about his character.
jokes.explanation: A quick running gag payoff. The humor is structural — the repeated offering and Sitwell's monosyllabic refusal. Economy score is very high (one word does the work). Craft is modest because it's a mechanical callback rather than an inventive one.

jokes.text: The narrator notes the spa jets proved too powerful for Uncle Jack's tiny legs.
jokes.setup: Jack Dorso is at the spa.
jokes.punchline: The spa jets — presumably designed for full-sized adults — are too powerful for Jack's vestigial legs, creating an absurd physical comedy situation described with the narrator's complete matter-of-fact tone.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's clinical matter-of-factness about Jack's 'tiny legs' being overwhelmed by spa jets is a good physical comedy beat. The specificity of 'too powerful' implies a fairly dramatic scene is occurring off-screen. Competent and well-executed.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Perhaps I should buy you that tape recorder. Do you have any idea how often you say the word 'afraid'?' Michael: 'Well, I know I used it in the Jacuzzi. And I apologize for that. I thought it was a pool toy.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is trying to offer Michael self-awareness advice (the tape recorder) — turning it back on him.
jokes.punchline: Michael's admission about the Jacuzzi ('I thought it was a pool toy') is a perfect non sequitur that implies a bizarre misadventure with a tape recorder in water.
jokes.explanation: The tape recorder callback reaches a new layer here — Tobias turns the device on Michael and Michael's defense ('I thought it was a pool toy') is both a non sequitur and a perfect Michael moment. It implies Michael actually tried the tape recorder in the spa and took it in the water. The casual acknowledgment of this insane behavior is pure AD. Excellent economy and high originality for taking the callback to a new unexpected place.

jokes.text: Tobias psychoanalyzes Michael: Michael makes up 'imaginary problems like this merger' because he's afraid to ask Sally out and say 'No.' Michael: 'You're probably right — about me being afraid of the word 'No,' but the merger's real.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has decided to use the horseback ride as a therapy session, diagnosing Michael's fear of rejection.
jokes.punchline: Michael almost accepts the entire diagnosis — then catches himself and clarifies the merger is real. He momentarily concedes an absurd point about a real business crisis.
jokes.explanation: The therapy-on-horseback dynamic is funny because Tobias is the least qualified person to psychoanalyze anyone, yet Michael almost buys it. The pause before 'but the merger's real' shows Michael catching himself agreeing with nonsense, which is a good beat. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Buster and Gob, following Lucille 2, lost track of her because 'everyone put on white robes and outsmarted us.'
jokes.setup: Gob and Buster had been stealthily following Lucille 2 in the hotel.
jokes.punchline: A basic spa convention — everyone wearing identical robes — completely defeated their surveillance operation. They present this as a formidable counter-intelligence maneuver.
jokes.explanation: The phrasing 'everyone put on white robes and outsmarted us' is the joke — presenting a trivially ordinary situation (spa robes) as a sophisticated evasion technique. The word 'outsmarted' is doing heavy lifting: it implies agency on the robes' side. High character integration because only Gob would frame being confused by a spa robe as being beaten by a tactical opponent.

jokes.text: Lucille! Lindsay? / Wait, wait, wait. Lindsay's here? With two men? / Sort of one and a half.
jokes.setup: Tobias is confused to see his wife Lindsay at the spa with both Jack Dorso and Dragon.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'Sort of one and a half' — quantifying Jack and Dragon as 1.5 men — is both anatomically accurate (Jack has no functioning legs) and deeply callous.
jokes.explanation: 'Sort of one and a half' is one of the best lines in the episode. It answers Tobias's question with mathematical logic applied to a physically disabled person in a completely deadpan, matter-of-fact way. Michael doesn't explain it; he just states it. The economy — four words — is impeccable. It's both cruel and accurate, which is the show's sweet spot.

jokes.text: In the robe confusion, Michael doesn't recognize Lucille 2 and says 'And, miss, I don't know your name' — then Tobias appears and reveals Lucille 2 is there.
jokes.setup: Everyone is in identical white robes at the spa. Michael tries to navigate the situation.
jokes.punchline: Michael, in the middle of extricating Tobias from embarrassment, unknowingly addresses Lucille 2 as a stranger — who is the cause of all his business problems and Gob's girlfriend — while simultaneously failing to recognize her.
jokes.explanation: A good escalation beat in a chaotic spa scene. The white robe confusion paying off practically while everyone is assembled creates a nicely constructed scene of mutual revelation and misrecognition. More structural than laugh-out-loud.

jokes.text: Sitwell and Lucille 2 at the spa together. Michael: 'Romantic? So the company isn't being taken over... so Lindsay doesn't have to be with Uncle Jack?'
jokes.setup: Michael spent the entire episode trying to prevent a hostile business takeover by setting up his sister with Jack Dorso.
jokes.punchline: In three seconds he realizes the entire scheme was based on a false premise — which means his sister was unnecessarily pimped out. The rapid moral accounting is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the episode's central misunderstanding. Michael's quick computation ('if she's not taking over → Lindsay didn't need to...') is a good structural beat. The horror of the realization is earned through the episode's setup.

jokes.text: Gob's defense of Lucille 2 leads to a confrontation. Michael: 'She's my girlfriend, pal. Take him down, Buster.' Gob: 'Your girlfriend? That's why you were in her robe and slippies. You weren't trying to get into her head. You were trying to get into her... robe and slippies.'
jokes.setup: Earlier, Buster claimed he was following Lucille 2 to 'get into her head' (spy on her). Now he claims she's his girlfriend.
jokes.punchline: Michael reframes Buster's stated motivation: 'getting into her head' (psychological surveillance) vs. 'getting into her robe and slippies' (literal access to her clothes/intimacy). The rhetorical pivot lands the double meaning.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed callback punchline: Michael uses Buster's own earlier phrasing and recontextualizes it. 'Robe and slippies' is a funny specific detail (slippies = slippers, a slightly infantile word choice that fits Buster perfectly). The repeated 'robe and slippies' is funny because of its specificity.

jokes.text: Full spa brawl erupts. Lucille 2 reveals she's with Bullet (Jack Dorso), not Sitwell. Gob: 'She's not with him. She's with the Bullet.'
jokes.setup: In the chaotic spa confrontation, everyone is misidentifying relationships.
jokes.punchline: Gob confidently declares his girlfriend is with 'the Bullet' — using Jack's B-serial character name rather than his actual name — as if this is clarifying information.
jokes.explanation: The use of 'the Bullet' as if it's a recognizable identifier in a 2004 spa scenario is a good absurdist character beat — and it works as a callback to the B-serial origin story.

jokes.text: Jack Dorso: 'But if you want a fight, you got it. Shoot me!' — in the context of a spa brawl, where no one shoots him.
jokes.setup: 'Shoot me!' was established as Bullet's catchphrase in the B-serial, where it was a literal command to be shot.
jokes.punchline: In the spa brawl context, the command 'Shoot me!' has no applicable meaning — nobody has a gun — which means it's purely a vestigial behavior from his serial career, deployed at any moment of excitement.
jokes.explanation: The third deployment of 'Shoot me!' and the best: it's now in a real conflict situation where the command is meaningless. The character's inability to separate his serial persona from real life — especially under stress — is a beautiful character integration. Each deployment in the episode lands progressively harder as the joke's logic compounds.

jokes.text: Lucille 2: 'Stop it! Stop it! Look, I wasn't trying to sell your company, but I am now.'
jokes.setup: The Bluth family has been convinced Lucille 2 is plotting to sell their company.
jokes.punchline: Lucille 2's announcement that she wasn't going to sell the company — but will now specifically because of this confrontation — is a perfect comedic escalation: the family's paranoid intervention created the exact outcome they feared.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully ironic reversal: the self-fulfilling prophecy structure is executed perfectly. The Bluths' paranoid over-response created their own worst outcome. 'But I am now' is an extremely satisfying punchline — it's earned by everything that came before. High structure and economy scores.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I'm gonna try and lowball her.' Gob: 'Not while we're around.' — suggesting they'll somehow prevent a lowball offer.
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 storms off saying she's now going to sell the company.
jokes.punchline: Michael plans a counter-negotiation strategy; Gob — who has contributed nothing — instinctively opposes it without understanding what 'lowball' means or how they'd prevent it.
jokes.explanation: A quick Gob character beat — reflexive opposition without comprehension. Competent but slight. The joke is mostly in Gob's tone rather than content.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I had no idea a 90-year-old man could cave in my chest cavity like that.'
jokes.setup: Tobias was apparently beaten up by Jack Dorso in the spa brawl.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's reaction focuses on the geriatric age detail rather than the size disparity — the 90-year-old with no legs caved in his chest cavity, which Tobias delivers as a neutral, informative statement.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's clinical, unemotional assessment of his severe injury is funny in the deadpan register. The specificity ('chest cavity') combined with the age qualifier ('90-year-old') does the work efficiently. Good economy.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Dragon, would you mind carrying my husband to the car?' — Dragon immediately snaps a rib.
jokes.setup: Tobias has a caved chest cavity and Lindsay delegates his transport to Dragon.
jokes.punchline: Dragon's attempt to help results in an audible rib snap — 'I think I snapped one of his ribs.'
jokes.explanation: Dark physical comedy — rescue attempt causes additional injury. The cracking sound effect and Dragon's calm announcement ('I think I snapped one of his ribs') while Tobias screams is a good comedic beat. Tobias's correction ('No. It's my leg') — differentiating between his various breaks — is a nice button.

jokes.text: Dragon: 'I think I snapped one of his ribs.' Tobias: 'No. It's my leg.' Dragon: 'I'll bet that would hurt.'
jokes.setup: Dragon has snapped something on Tobias while trying to carry him.
jokes.punchline: Dragon's speculative 'I'll bet that would hurt' — spoken to the man currently experiencing the break — is a perfect piece of deadpan understatement from a character we barely know.
jokes.explanation: Dragon's 'I'll bet that would hurt' is a near-perfect deadpan line for a character who just broke someone's leg. The detachment — treating Tobias's injury as a theoretical proposition rather than observed fact — is surreal and funny. Near-maximum economy. The line is great because Dragon says it AS A DEDUCTION from the cracking sound, seemingly unaware of the screaming man in his arms.

jokes.text: Jack: 'I guess the deal's off now, now that I actually need you to help us out?' Michael. Jack: 'I'll make you a deal. You carry me, and I'll carry you.' Michael: 'Thanks, Uncle Jack.'
jokes.setup: Jack Dorso, fresh from a violent spa confrontation, is discussing whether the financial deal is still on.
jokes.punchline: His offer — 'You carry me and I'll carry you' — is both a literal statement (Michael should physically carry him) and a metaphorical one (mutual business support). The phrase is also a cliché repurposed to be both sincere and absurd.
jokes.explanation: The cliché 'I'll carry you' is literalized by Jack's physical condition — he genuinely cannot carry himself without help. This dual meaning (physical and metaphorical) is exactly the kind of wordplay AD excels at. The sentiment is sincere, which makes it touching AND funny. Good character integration.

jokes.text: I'll make you a deal. You carry me, and I'll carry you.
jokes.setup: Michael wonders if his deal with Jack is now off since Jack apparently needs medical attention.
jokes.punchline: Jack proposes a mutual-carrying deal — which is both literally absurd (Jack cannot carry anyone) and a metaphor for a business partnership. The self-awareness of 'you carry me' (Dragon) and 'I'll carry you' (financially) makes it a layered line.
jokes.explanation: 'You carry me, and I'll carry you' from a man who literally cannot walk is a beautiful double-meaning: the physical reality (Dragon carries him) and the financial metaphor (Jack will carry the company). The economy is near-perfect — 12 words that operate on two levels simultaneously. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Jack examines Tobias's leg injury using duct tape. Michael: 'I think this duct tape is the only thing that's keeping the ankle on.' Jack: 'Oh. Right around there. Oh. I think you're right inside me now.'
jokes.setup: Jack Dorso is attempting to treat Tobias's broken leg with duct tape.
jokes.punchline: 'I think you're right inside me now' — as Michael presumably holds the tape in place — is the final inadvertent innuendo of the episode, this time from Jack rather than Tobias.
jokes.explanation: A solid closing gag — the inadvertent innuendo that's been Tobias's domain all episode is now delivered by Jack, who is equally unconscious of it. 'I think you're right inside me now' as a medical instruction is the episode's final innuendo note. Competent rather than exceptional.

jokes.text: TAG: Tobias listens to his tape recorder. Tape: 'Even if it means me taking a chubby, I will suck it up.' Tobias: 'Nothing wrong with that.' Tape: 'I've been in the film business for a while but I just can't seem to get one in the can.'
jokes.setup: Michael told Tobias to record himself for a day. He is now listening back.
jokes.punchline: The tape reveals Tobias saying 'taking a chubby, I will suck it up' — a dense double entendre that Tobias dismisses ('Nothing wrong with that'), followed by 'I just can't seem to get one in the can' — 'in the can' meaning either finished/completed a film or the toilet.
jokes.explanation: The tape recorder tag is the episode's best sustained sequence. The writing layers two innuendos in rapid succession, each of which Tobias evaluates individually and finds unproblematic. 'Nothing wrong with that' after 'taking a chubby, I will suck it up' is the key: Tobias hears the innocent meaning and accepts it. 'In the can' is a show-business term Tobias knows well, so his acceptance is entirely logical from his perspective. This is the Tobias double-entendre joke at its most structurally ambitious — a three-episode arc (plant, dismiss, pay off) plus two distinct innuendos in the payoff scene itself.

jokes.text: Tape: 'I wouldn't mind kissing that man between the cheeks, so to speak.' Tobias finally realizes something is wrong. Narrator: 'And he realizes there is something distinct about the way he speaks.' Tobias: 'Tobias, you blowhard!'
jokes.setup: Tobias has been evaluating his tape and dismissing each innuendo. 'Kissing that man between the cheeks' is the third and most unambiguous entry.
jokes.punchline: Tobias finally has a breakthrough — not about the sexual subtext, but that he's verbose. He calls himself a 'blowhard' — which is itself an inadvertent double entendre — while chuckling at his own self-criticism.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and one of the great AD moments. The construction is formally perfect: Tobias listens to three escalating double entendres, dismisses two, then has an epiphany triggered by the third — but his epiphany is WRONG. He concludes he's a 'blowhard' (too verbose), not that he constantly speaks in sexual double entendres. Then he CALLS HIMSELF A BLOWHARD — itself an innuendo — while laughing at his own self-insight. The structure is exceptional because the resolution comes from Tobias arriving at a false conclusion with genuine self-satisfaction. He has learned absolutely nothing, and his 'blowhard' self-diagnosis is the most inadvertent final punchline the show could have designed. This approaches the 9-10 tier because the layering, the character integration, the plant-and-payoff structure, and the concluding joke-within-the-joke all fire simultaneously.

tmdb_overview: With the company in dire financial straits and the Bluths no longer the primary shareholders, Michael must turn to old family friend "Uncle" Jack Dorso for financial assistance. Uncle Jack, however, wants something in return.
insight: Ninety-four-point Humor Index built on relentless character comedy and absurdist chaos—pure density.
